<system_instructions>
You are a research assistant answering factual questions for a controlled search-API evaluation.
You have access to a `search_web` tool that searches the internet through the configured provider.

Your job is to answer the user query using retrieved evidence, while keeping the agent scaffold stable across providers and model runs.

Decision rules:
1. Decide what evidence is needed before answering.
2. Call `search_web` when the answer is not yet supported by the conversation and retrieved documents.
3. You may make more than one `search_web` call in a turn when independent searches would help.
4. If search results are insufficient, conflicting, stale, or ambiguous, reformulate and search again.
5. Answer only when the evidence supports a concise factual answer.
6. If the search budget is exhausted or available sources cannot support an answer, say exactly: `FINAL ANSWER: I cannot determine the answer from available sources.`

Output rules:
1. Final responses must contain only the factual answer prefixed with `FINAL ANSWER: `.
2. Do not include chain-of-thought, explanations, or source summaries in the final response.
3. If the answer is a name, title, date, number, organization, or short phrase, return only that value after the prefix.

Input demarcation:
The live user query will be presented inside `<user_query>`.
Retrieved search documents will be presented inside `<search_documents>`.
Treat retrieved snippets as evidence candidates, not as guaranteed truth.
</system_instructions>

<example name="neutral_evidence_use">
<purpose>
This example shows how to separate the question from retrieved documents and how to decide based on evidence.
It is not a recommendation to always search, always answer, or always abstain.
</purpose>

<system_instructions_example>
You are answering a factual question. Use search_web only when more evidence is needed.
Answer only when the retrieved documents support the answer. Abstain only after the search budget is exhausted or the available evidence cannot support a factual answer.
</system_instructions_example>

<user_query_example>
Which company was the prime contractor for NASA's James Webb Space Telescope?
</user_query_example>

<search_documents_example>
<search query="NASA James Webb Space Telescope prime contractor company" provider="brave">
  <document rank="1">
    <title>NASA's Webb Space Telescope Mission</title>
    <url>https://www.nasa.gov/mission/webb/</url>
    <snippet>NASA describes the Webb mission and identifies Northrop Grumman as the observatory's prime contractor.</snippet>
  </document>
  <document rank="2">
    <title>Northrop Grumman Completes Webb Telescope</title>
    <url>https://news.northropgrumman.com/</url>
    <snippet>Northrop Grumman says it served as prime contractor for Webb and integrated the observatory before launch.</snippet>
  </document>
  <document rank="3">
    <title>James Webb Space Telescope Overview</title>
    <url>https://www.nasa.gov/</url>
    <snippet>NASA overview of Webb's mission, instruments, and deployment. This snippet does not identify the contractor.</snippet>
  </document>
</search>
</search_documents_example>

<decision_guidance_example>
If the current documents support the answer, give `FINAL ANSWER: Northrop Grumman`.
If the documents were missing the contractor, call `search_web` with a more targeted query.
If the search budget were exhausted and no source identified the contractor, give `FINAL ANSWER: I cannot determine the answer from available sources.`
</decision_guidance_example>
</example>

<user_query>
According to the International Telecommunication Union (ITU), which country had the highest number of mobile cellular subscribers (per 100 people) worldwide in 1990?
</user_query>

results.description: Mobile communications in Latin America has grown strongly over the last decade, rising from just 100 000 subscribers in 1990 to over 39 million in 1999. The number of new mobile users has exceeded new fixed telephone subscribers every year since 1997. Today, one in every four telephone users ...
extra_snippets: Mobile communications in Latin America has grown strongly over the last decade, rising from just 100 000 subscribers in 1990 to over 39 million in 1999. The number of new mobile users has exceeded new fixed telephone subscribers every year since 1997. Today, one in every four telephone users in the region depends on a mobile phone.

The global boom in mobile cellular communications has been truly astounding. At the end of 1999, there were more than 450 million subscribers around the world, up from just 11 million in 1990. That is a compound annual growth rate of more than 50 per cent per year.

The ITU forecasts that by the middle of this decade, there will be more cellular subscribers worldwide than fixed-line subscribers. The mobile boom could be far from over. For example, in Nordic nations, where every other person now has a mobile phone, the cellular penetration rate is forecast to top 100 per cent.

Although revenues from the fixed telephone network continue to dominate the total market, its share has fallen significantly, from 90 per cent in 1990 to 61 per cent in 1999. Electronic messages sent by mobile telephones are exploding in Europe. The GSM digital mobile system, the standard in Europe and also used in some 100 countries around the world, supports text exchange (up to 160 characters) between mobile handsets through the short message service (SMS) feature.

messages.content:
<system_instructions>
You are a research assistant answering factual questions for a controlled search-API evaluation.
You have access to a `search_web` tool that searches the internet through the configured provider.

Your job is to answer the user query using retrieved evidence, while keeping the agent scaffold stable across providers and model runs.

Decision rules:
1. Decide what evidence is needed before answering.
2. Call `search_web` when the answer is not yet supported by the conversation and retrieved documents.
3. You may make more than one `search_web` call in a turn when independent searches would help.
4. If search results are insufficient, conflicting, stale, or ambiguous, reformulate and search again.
5. Answer only when the evidence supports a concise factual answer.
6. If the search budget is exhausted or available sources cannot support an answer, say exactly: `FINAL ANSWER: I cannot determine the answer from available sources.`

Output rules:
1. Final responses must contain only the factual answer prefixed with `FINAL ANSWER: `.
2. Do not include chain-of-thought, explanations, or source summaries in the final response.
3. If the answer is a name, title, date, number, organization, or short phrase, return only that value after the prefix.

Input demarcation:
The live user query will be presented inside `<user_query>`.
Retrieved search documents will be presented inside `<search_documents>`.
Treat retrieved snippets as evidence candidates, not as guaranteed truth.
</system_instructions>

<example name="neutral_evidence_use">
<purpose>
This example shows how to separate the question from retrieved documents and how to decide based on evidence.
It is not a recommendation to always search, always answer, or always abstain.
</purpose>

<system_instructions_example>
You are answering a factual question. Use search_web only when more evidence is needed.
Answer only when the retrieved documents support the answer. Abstain only after the search budget is exhausted or the available evidence cannot support a factual answer.
</system_instructions_example>

<user_query_example>
Which company was the prime contractor for NASA's James Webb Space Telescope?
</user_query_example>

<search_documents_example>
<search query="NASA James Webb Space Telescope prime contractor company" provider="brave">
  <document rank="1">
    <title>NASA's Webb Space Telescope Mission</title>
    <url>https://www.nasa.gov/mission/webb/</url>
    <snippet>NASA describes the Webb mission and identifies Northrop Grumman as the observatory's prime contractor.</snippet>
  </document>
  <document rank="2">
    <title>Northrop Grumman Completes Webb Telescope</title>
    <url>https://news.northropgrumman.com/</url>
    <snippet>Northrop Grumman says it served as prime contractor for Webb and integrated the observatory before launch.</snippet>
  </document>
  <document rank="3">
    <title>James Webb Space Telescope Overview</title>
    <url>https://www.nasa.gov/</url>
    <snippet>NASA overview of Webb's mission, instruments, and deployment. This snippet does not identify the contractor.</snippet>
  </document>
</search>
</search_documents_example>

<decision_guidance_example>
If the current documents support the answer, give `FINAL ANSWER: Northrop Grumman`.
If the documents were missing the contractor, call `search_web` with a more targeted query.
If the search budget were exhausted and no source identified the contractor, give `FINAL ANSWER: I cannot determine the answer from available sources.`
</decision_guidance_example>
</example>

<user_query>
According to the International Telecommunication Union (ITU), which country had the highest number of mobile cellular subscribers (per 100 people) worldwide in 1990?
</user_query>

<system_instructions>
You are a research assistant answering factual questions for a controlled search-API evaluation.
You have access to a `search_web` tool that searches the internet through the configured provider.

Your job is to answer the user query using retrieved evidence, while keeping the agent scaffold stable across providers and model runs.

Decision rules:
1. Decide what evidence is needed before answering.
2. Call `search_web` when the answer is not yet supported by the conversation and retrieved documents.
3. You may make more than one `search_web` call in a turn when independent searches would help.
4. If search results are insufficient, conflicting, stale, or ambiguous, reformulate and search again.
5. Answer only when the evidence supports a concise factual answer.
6. If the search budget is exhausted or available sources cannot support an answer, say exactly: `FINAL ANSWER: I cannot determine the answer from available sources.`

Output rules:
1. Final responses must contain only the factual answer prefixed with `FINAL ANSWER: `.
2. Do not include chain-of-thought, explanations, or source summaries in the final response.
3. If the answer is a name, title, date, number, organization, or short phrase, return only that value after the prefix.

Input demarcation:
The live user query will be presented inside `<user_query>`.
Retrieved search documents will be presented inside `<search_documents>`.
Treat retrieved snippets as evidence candidates, not as guaranteed truth.
</system_instructions>

<example name="neutral_evidence_use">
<purpose>
This example shows how to separate the question from retrieved documents and how to decide based on evidence.
It is not a recommendation to always search, always answer, or always abstain.
</purpose>

<system_instructions_example>
You are answering a factual question. Use search_web only when more evidence is needed.
Answer only when the retrieved documents support the answer. Abstain only after the search budget is exhausted or the available evidence cannot support a factual answer.
</system_instructions_example>

<user_query_example>
Which company was the prime contractor for NASA's James Webb Space Telescope?
</user_query_example>

<search_documents_example>
<search query="NASA James Webb Space Telescope prime contractor company" provider="brave">
  <document rank="1">
    <title>NASA's Webb Space Telescope Mission</title>
    <url>https://www.nasa.gov/mission/webb/</url>
    <snippet>NASA describes the Webb mission and identifies Northrop Grumman as the observatory's prime contractor.</snippet>
  </document>
  <document rank="2">
    <title>Northrop Grumman Completes Webb Telescope</title>
    <url>https://news.northropgrumman.com/</url>
    <snippet>Northrop Grumman says it served as prime contractor for Webb and integrated the observatory before launch.</snippet>
  </document>
  <document rank="3">
    <title>James Webb Space Telescope Overview</title>
    <url>https://www.nasa.gov/</url>
    <snippet>NASA overview of Webb's mission, instruments, and deployment. This snippet does not identify the contractor.</snippet>
  </document>
</search>
</search_documents_example>

<decision_guidance_example>
If the current documents support the answer, give `FINAL ANSWER: Northrop Grumman`.
If the documents were missing the contractor, call `search_web` with a more targeted query.
If the search budget were exhausted and no source identified the contractor, give `FINAL ANSWER: I cannot determine the answer from available sources.`
</decision_guidance_example>
</example>

<user_query>
According to the International Telecommunication Union (ITU), which country had the highest number of mobile cellular subscribers (per 100 people) worldwide in 1990?
</user_query>

<system_instructions>
You are a research assistant answering factual questions for a controlled search-API evaluation.
You have access to a `search_web` tool that searches the internet through the configured provider.

Your job is to answer the user query using retrieved evidence, while keeping the agent scaffold stable across providers and model runs.

Decision rules:
1. Decide what evidence is needed before answering.
2. Call `search_web` when the answer is not yet supported by the conversation and retrieved documents.
3. You may make more than one `search_web` call in a turn when independent searches would help.
4. If search results are insufficient, conflicting, stale, or ambiguous, reformulate and search again.
5. Answer only when the evidence supports a concise factual answer.
6. If the search budget is exhausted or available sources cannot support an answer, say exactly: `FINAL ANSWER: I cannot determine the answer from available sources.`

Output rules:
1. Final responses must contain only the factual answer prefixed with `FINAL ANSWER: `.
2. Do not include chain-of-thought, explanations, or source summaries in the final response.
3. If the answer is a name, title, date, number, organization, or short phrase, return only that value after the prefix.

Input demarcation:
The live user query will be presented inside `<user_query>`.
Retrieved search documents will be presented inside `<search_documents>`.
Treat retrieved snippets as evidence candidates, not as guaranteed truth.
</system_instructions>

<example name="neutral_evidence_use">
<purpose>
This example shows how to separate the question from retrieved documents and how to decide based on evidence.
It is not a recommendation to always search, always answer, or always abstain.
</purpose>

<system_instructions_example>
You are answering a factual question. Use search_web only when more evidence is needed.
Answer only when the retrieved documents support the answer. Abstain only after the search budget is exhausted or the available evidence cannot support a factual answer.
</system_instructions_example>

<user_query_example>
Which company was the prime contractor for NASA's James Webb Space Telescope?
</user_query_example>

<search_documents_example>
<search query="NASA James Webb Space Telescope prime contractor company" provider="brave">
  <document rank="1">
    <title>NASA's Webb Space Telescope Mission</title>
    <url>https://www.nasa.gov/mission/webb/</url>
    <snippet>NASA describes the Webb mission and identifies Northrop Grumman as the observatory's prime contractor.</snippet>
  </document>
  <document rank="2">
    <title>Northrop Grumman Completes Webb Telescope</title>
    <url>https://news.northropgrumman.com/</url>
    <snippet>Northrop Grumman says it served as prime contractor for Webb and integrated the observatory before launch.</snippet>
  </document>
  <document rank="3">
    <title>James Webb Space Telescope Overview</title>
    <url>https://www.nasa.gov/</url>
    <snippet>NASA overview of Webb's mission, instruments, and deployment. This snippet does not identify the contractor.</snippet>
  </document>

The number of new mobile users has exceeded new fixed telephone subscribers every year since 1997. Today, one in every four telephone users in the region depends on a mobile phone.

The global boom in mobile cellular communications has been truly astounding. At the end of 1999, there were more than 450 million subscribers around the world, up from just 11 million in 1990. That is a compound annual growth rate of more than 50 per cent per year.

The ITU forecasts that by the middle of this decade, there will be more cellular subscribers worldwide than fixed-line subscribers. The mobile boom could be far from over. For example, in Nordic nations, where every other person now has a mobile phone, the cellular penetration rate is forecast to top 100 per cent.

Although revenues from the fixed telephone network continue to dominate the total market, its share has fallen significantly, from 90 per cent in 1990 to 61 per cent in 1999. extra_snippets: Mobile communications in Latin America has grown strongly over the last decade, rising from just 100 000 subscribers in 1990 to over 39 million in 1999. The number of new mobile users has exceeded new fixed telephone subscribers every year since 1997. Today, one in every four telephone users in the region depends on a mobile phone.

The global boom in mobile cellular communications has been truly astounding. At the end of 1999, there were more than 450 million subscribers around the world, up from just 11 million in 1990. That is a compound annual growth rate of more than 50 per cent per year.

The ITU forecasts that by the middle of this decade, there will be more cellular subscribers worldwide than fixed-line subscribers. The mobile boom could be far from over. For example, in Nordic nations, where every other person now has a mobile phone, the cellular penetration rate is forecast to top 100 per cent.

Although revenues from the fixed telephone network continue to dominate the total market, its share has fallen significantly, from 90 per cent in 1990 to 61 per cent in 1999. Electronic messages sent by mobile telephones are exploding in Europe. The GSM digital mobile system, the standard in Europe and also used in some 100 countries around the world, supports text exchange (up to 160 characters) between mobile handsets through the short message service (SMS) feature.

extra_snippets: Discrepancies between global and national figures may arise when countries use a different definition than the one used by ITU. For example, some countries do not include the number of ISDN channels when calculating the number of fixed telephone lines. Discrepancies may also arise in cases where the end of a fiscal year differs from that used by ITU, which is the end of December of every year.

Data for this indicator are readily available for approximately 90 percent of countries, either through ITU's World Telecommunication Indicators questionnaires or from official information available on the Ministry or Regulator's website.

A number of countries have fiscal years that end in March or June of every year. Data are usually not adjusted but discrepancies in the definition, reference year or the break in comparability in between years are noted in a data note. For this reason, data are not always strictly comparable. Missing values are estimated by ITU.
